question: Explain the difference between a memory-mapped I/O scheme and an isolated I/O scheme. What is one major advantage and one disadvantage of the memory-mapped approach?
expectedPoints: In memory-mapped I/O, I/O devices and their registers are mapped into the main memory address space, meaning the same instructions used for memory access (e.g., LOAD, STORE) are used for I/O.
In isolated I/O, I/O devices have a separate address space from memory, requiring special instructions (e.g., IN, OUT) to access them.
An advantage of memory-mapped I/O is that it simplifies the instruction set and allows the use of a full set of memory instructions for data manipulation on I/O devices.
A disadvantage is that it reduces the amount of memory address space available for actual RAM, as a portion is dedicated to I/O addresses.

question: What is the purpose of a subroutine linkage convention, such as saving registers on a stack? Describe the two key steps a CALL instruction must perform.
expectedPoints: The purpose is to preserve the state of the calling program (e.g., register contents) so the subroutine can use processor resources without corrupting the caller's data.
The CALL instruction must first save the return address (the address of the next instruction) so the program knows where to resume after the subroutine finishes.
Second, the CALL instruction transfers control to the starting address of the subroutine by loading that address into the program counter (PC).

question: Describe, in detail, the complete fetch-decode-execute cycle for a typical RISC-style LOAD instruction (e.g., lw $t0, 8($s1)). Your answer should cover the role of the program counter, instruction register, memory, and the ALU across multiple clock cycles.
expectedPoints: Fetch: The PC holds the address of the instruction. This address is sent to memory, which returns the instruction code. This code is placed in the Instruction Register (IR), and the PC is incremented to the next instruction address.

Decode: The contents of the IR are decoded to determine the operation (LOAD), the source register ($s1), the destination register ($t0), and the offset (8). The contents of the source register are read from the register file.
Execute/Calculate Address: The ALU is used to calculate the effective memory address by adding the contents of the source register ($s1) to the sign-extended offset (8).
Memory Access: The calculated effective address is sent to the memory unit. The data value at that address is fetched from memory.
Write-Back: The data value retrieved from memory is written into the destination register ($t0) in the register file.
